ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for smoked goose breast with black pepper. FOODON_03311434 (smoked poultry food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Goose meat) is the closest parent; no specific smoked goose breast with black pepper entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for smoked goose breast with black pepper. SNOMED CT coverage of specific processed poultry preparations is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of black pepper smoked goose breast is approximately 150 g, presented as sliced smoked goose breast seasoned with cracked black pepper.

nutrient_source.title: Goose, domesticated, meat only, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05143)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for smoked goose breast with black pepper exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked goose meat (NDB 05143). Smoking typically reduces moisture and concentrates protein and fat slightly; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect curing and smoking. Black pepper contributes negligible macronutrients at typical seasoning quantities. Adaptation is conservative and documented.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat and poultry products contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (0.5 g per 100 g) and are conventionally assigned a GI of 0 (effectively non-glycemic). Per Veritas v2 rules, glycemic_index must be a numeric value greater than zero, so it is set to 1. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables confirm that protein- and fat-dominant foods with very low carbohydrate content are classified as low GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (1 × 0.75) / 100 = 0.0075, rounded to 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from smoking and curing process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid smoked goose breast due to its elevated sodium content (approximately 680 mg per 100 g), which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake and raise blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black pepper smoked goose breast has a negligible carbohydrate content (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g) and a GI of effectively zero, making it unlikely to cause postprandial blood glucose spikes; however, portion size should be moderated due to its moderate fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from smoking and curing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (approximately 3.9 g per 100 g) and high sodium from the smoking and curing process present a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this food occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Smoked Goose Breast (Vietnamese: Lườn ngỗng xông khói tiêu đen; Chinese: 黑椒烟熏鹅胸肉) is a processed poultry dish featuring goose breast meat that has been cured, smoked, and generously seasoned with cracked black pepper. The dish is of Vietnamese culinary origin and is served in Vietnamese restaurants operating in Singapore, such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The smoking process imparts a deep, savory, and slightly woody aroma to the meat, while the black pepper crust adds pungency and mild heat. The texture is firm yet tender, with a characteristic smoky chew. Nutritionally, smoked goose breast is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with negligible carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The smoking and curing process significantly elevates sodium content, which is a clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The saturated fat content is moderate and warrants attention for those with dyslipidaemia. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is typically served as a cold appetiser or as part of a shared platter in Vietnamese-style dining.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for smoked goose breast with black pepper was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA Branded Foods. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked goose meat (NDB 05143) with conservative adjustments for the smoking and seasoning process. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically sound but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific product.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for cooked goose meat. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for cooked goose meat. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked goose meat — naturally zero for this poultry product. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0; naturally zero as this is a pure meat product with no plant-derived vitamin C. Sugar (sugar_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data — goose meat contains no added or intrinsic sugars. Glycemic index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (0.5 g per 100 g); gi_category is low per convention for protein- and fat-dominant foods as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0, calculated from GI=1 and carbohydrate per serving of 0.75 g. Sodium is adjusted upward from the USDA base value to approximately 680 mg per 100 g to reflect the curing and smoking process typical of this preparation. The food is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant on Circular Road, Singapore (GrabFood Singapore source). Halal status is not confirmed as goose sourcing and slaughter method are unknown. Dietary fibre (0.2 g) reflects a minor contribution from black pepper seasoning.
